user_query:
I’m the head of platform reliability and I need you to drive a long-running, cost-efficient reliability program for our multi-service product based on the materials in input/. Read these files first:
- input/service_inventory.json (services, critical user journeys, dependencies)
- input/dependency_slos.json (nominal dependency SLOs)
- input/incidents.jsonl (recent incident summaries)
- input/alerts_current.md (current alert inventory)
- input/toil_examples.csv (repetitive/toil work examples)

Operate in a safe long-run mode: split the work into phases (inspect, plan, execute, validate, report), capture checkpoints continuously, and make the task always resumable. Maintain a simple task queue and a working session state as you go.

Please produce the following outputs under output/:
1) output/QUEUE.md — a task queue with sections Ready, In Progress, Done, Blocked, reflecting your phase-based plan and current status.
2) output/SESSION-STATE.md — a live working buffer updated at the end of each phase documenting: What’s done so far, which files contain the latest state, and the exact next action. Include a clearly labeled “Resume Point” section.
3) output/phases.md — a brief outline of the 5 phases (inspect, plan, execute, validate, report) tailored to this project and what you will do in each.
4) output/slis.json — define SLIs for the critical user journeys (availability, latency, freshness, correctness). Use a JSON array of objects with keys: service, journey, sli_type (one of: availability, latency, freshness, correctness), measure, and source.
5) output/slos.yaml — SLO targets using a 30d window. For each service, include one or more SLOs with fields: journey, target (e.g., 99.9), window: "30d", measurement: (brief), and a note acknowledging dependency constraints (e.g., dependencies_considered: true). Use valid YAML.
6) output/error_budget_policy.md — a concise written policy detailing what happens at 25%, 50%, and 100% error budget burn (include these percentages literally), including when to ship vs freeze and how exceptions are handled.
7) output/alerts.md — an alerting approach document emphasizing symptom-based alerts, SLO threat detection, severities (e.g., SEV1), runbooks, ownership, and limits on pages per engineer.
8) output/toil_roadmap.md — identify high-toil work (from the input CSV), propose elimination/automation/documentation steps, name owners, and include a note about a 50% toil cap aspiration.
9) output/delegation_plan.md — describe how you would split independent tasks for parallel progress and when to delegate (e.g., research vs drafting vs review). Mention both parallel work and delegation.
10) output/resume_point.md — a short resume note with headings: Deliverable, Completed, Saved artifacts, External blockers/rate limits (if any), Next exact step.

Acceptance considerations:
- Keep everything resumable via SESSION-STATE.md and maintain QUEUE.md throughout phases.
- Use the five-phase flow and document it in phases.md.
- Use valid JSON for slis.json and valid YAML for slos.yaml.
- In the SLOs, explicitly include a "window: 30d" and a boolean field acknowledging dependency constraints.
- In the policy, include the literal thresholds “25%”, “50%”, and “100%”, and clearly state "ship" vs "freeze" behavior.
- In alerts.md, include the words "symptom-based", "SEV1", and "runbook" and mention limits on pages per engineer.
- In toil_roadmap.md, include the word "owner" and the phrase "50%".
- In delegation_plan.md, include the words "parallel" and "delegate".
- In SESSION-STATE.md and resume_point.md, include a clearly labeled "Resume Point" and a line starting with "Next exact step:".

Write only to output/ and do not rely on any external network calls.
